ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for Imarti. Mapped to broader deep-fried food product class. A more specific term for Indian syrup-soaked fried confections is absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sweet snacks and confectionery — deep-fried dough-based sweet
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Imarti or urad dal-based fried sweets. A0CKW (sweet snacks, deep-fried dough-based) used as closest defensible parent analogue. Imarti uses lentil batter rather than wheat dough but the preparation and product class are equivalent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Imarti or equivalent Indian fried sweet confection.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Imarti is one piece weighing approximately 60 g, equivalent to one medium-sized flower-shaped sweet as commonly sold at Indian sweet shops.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods does not list Imarti as a standalone entry. Values are adapted from the NIN database entry for Jalebi (urad dal-based fried sweet soaked in sugar syrup), which is the closest approved-source analogue. Protein, fat, carbohydrate, and energy values are consistent with the urad dal batter and sugar syrup composition described in the food record. Micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, iron, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, vitamins) are retained from the input enriched JSON at confidence 0.9 and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for similar fried lentil-based sweets. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 4.5 g per 100 g based on ghee/oil frying medium typical for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Imarti exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 68 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: Jalebi (deep-fried wheat/lentil batter soaked in sugar syrup), which has a reported GI of approximately 65–75 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008; Diabetes Care). The urad dal batter of Imarti provides slightly more protein and fibre than pure refined-flour Jalebi, which may marginally moderate the glycemic response; however, the dominant sugar syrup component drives a medium-to-high GI. GI set at 68 as a conservative medium estimate. Glycemic load per 60 g serving = (68 × 36.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 24.5 (high GL). Input record GI of 70 (high) noted; revised to 68 medium based on analogous-food evidence and lentil-batter moderating effect.

hypertension.key_factor: High caloric density from deep-frying and sugar syrup; moderate sodium content
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Imarti to occasional small portions, as its high caloric density and saturated fat content from deep-frying in ghee can contribute to weight gain and dyslipidaemia, both of which worsen blood pressure control; sodium content at 25 mg per 100 g is low but overall dietary pattern should be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar content (52 g per 100 g) and medium-to-high glycemic index (~68) causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Imarti is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high sugar content of 52 g per 100 g and an estimated glycemic index of 68, which will cause significant postprandial blood glucose spikes; if consumed at all, portion size should be strictly limited to a few bites and paired with a low-GI meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from deep-frying in ghee, high sugar load, and caloric density unfavourable for cardiovascular risk management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit Imarti, as the combination of approximately 4.5 g saturated fat per 100 g from ghee-based deep-frying and 52 g of sugar per 100 g contributes to elevated LDL cholesterol, triglycerides, and overall cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar and deep-fried fat content are pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Imarti as its high refined sugar and deep-fried fat content may exacerbate systemic inflammation and worsen joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Imarti is a traditional Indian deep-fried sweet confection made from a batter of urad dal (husked black gram lentils), which distinguishes it from the superficially similar Jalebi that uses refined wheat flour. The batter is piped through a mould or by hand into hot ghee or oil in an intricate flower-like pattern of concentric loops, then deep-fried until it achieves a soft yet slightly crisp exterior. The fried pieces are immediately immersed in warm sugar syrup flavoured with saffron and cardamom, which they absorb to become dense, chewy, and intensely sweet. Imarti has a characteristic amber-orange colour, a thick and chewy texture, and a pronounced nutty flavour from the lentil base that sets it apart from the tangier, lighter Jalebi. It is a festive staple across North India, particularly during Diwali, Dussehra, and weddings, and is equally popular in South India under the name Jangiri (Tamil) or Jangri (Telugu). Nutritionally, Imarti is energy-dense at approximately 385 kcal per 100 g, with 60 g carbohydrates (of which 52 g is sugar), 14.5 g fat, and 5.2 g protein. The high sugar and fat content make it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The urad dal base contributes modest amounts of protein, dietary fibre (1.8 g), folate (45 mcg), and iron (1.8 mg) compared to pure flour-based sweets. Glycemic index is estimated at approximately 68 (medium-to-high), with a glycemic load of approximately 24.5 per 60 g serving, indicating a significant postprandial glucose impact.
common_user_queries: Is Imarti the same as Jalebi?
What is the glycemic index of Imarti?
What is Imarti called in Tamil?
What is Imarti called in Telugu?
What festivals is Imarti associated with?
meal_context: festive sweet during Diwali and Dussehra

confidence_reason: No exact Imarti entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a standalone record. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest approved-source analogue (NIN Jalebi / urad dal fried sweet) and cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy data for similar preparations. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than high because exact-match source data is unavailable.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (Jalebi / urad dal fried sweet analogue) and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. No standalone Imarti entry found in any approved primary source. 2. Saturated fat estimated at 4.5 g per 100 g based on typical ghee/oil frying medium; monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g set to -1 as detailed fatty acid profile and moisture data for this traditional preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace for this preparation; no plant-derived vitamin C source is present in the final product after deep-frying and syrup soaking. 4. GI estimated at 68 (medium) based on analogous Jalebi data from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); input record GI of 70 revised to 68 to reflect lentil-batter moderating effect. Glycemic load per 60 g serving = 24.5 (high). 5. Portions deduplicated from input (four identical 60 g entries reduced to one canonical serving). 6. FoodOn mapped to broader deep-fried food product class (FOODON_03301710); no exact Imarti term exists. FoodEx2 code A0CKW used as closest defensible parent analogue for sweet deep-fried dough-based snack; foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' per allowed values. 7. Imarti is gluten-free as it uses urad dal batter rather than wheat flour; is_vegan set to false as ghee (clarified butter) is typically used in frying. 8. Input dietary_tag 'low_sodium' retained as sodium is 25 mg per 100 g, which is genuinely low.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Changed ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' — 'analogous_estimate' is not in the allowed set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']; 'closest_parent' is the correct allowed value for a non-exact analogue mapping.

Iteration 3: Confirmed foodex2_match_type is 'closest_parent' (allowed value). Updated foodex2_gap_note to clarify the closest_parent rationale. Updated notes field to document the correction. All other sections preserved from iteration 2.
